exercises.title: MASFT categories and the five recurring modes
exercises.prompt: MASFT (Berkeley 2025) catalogs 14 multi-agent failure modes in 3 categories. Name the three categories, and the five industry-recurring failure modes (hallucinated actions, scope creep, cascading errors, context loss, tool misuse). Why do agentic settings amplify existing AI failures?
exercises.hint: MASFT 3 categories: specification/design failures, inter-agent misalignment, task verification failures. Five recurring: hallucinated actions, scope creep, cascading errors, context loss, tool misuse. Amplify because agents take ACTIONS over many steps — a hallucination becomes a harmful action, errors compound across steps.
exercises.solution:
**MASFT's three failure categories (Berkeley 2025, 14 modes in 3 categories):**
1. **Specification / design failures:** failures from **how the multi-agent system was specified or designed** — unclear roles, bad task decomposition, ambiguous instructions, poor system structure. The failure is baked into the design (the agents do what they were (badly) told).
2. **Inter-agent misalignment:** failures from **agents not coordinating well** — agents working at cross-purposes, miscommunication between agents, conflicting actions, failure to share information. The agents individually might be fine but **don't align with each other**.
3. **Task verification failures:** failures in **verifying whether the task was actually done correctly** — agents marking work complete when it isn't, no/weak verification, accepting wrong results. The system **fails to check its own work** (the central reliability problem — the builder marking its own work done, Lessons 38-39).

**The five industry-recurring failure modes** (field data converges on these):
1. **Hallucinated actions:** the agent **takes an action based on a hallucination** — calls a tool that doesn't exist, uses made-up arguments, or acts on a fabricated belief (BFCL's hallucination category, Lesson 6). A hallucination becomes a *harmful action*, not just a wrong statement.
2. **Scope creep:** the agent **does more than it was asked** — drifts beyond the task boundary, modifies things it shouldn't, expands the work uncontrollably (the scope problem, Lesson 36).
3. **Cascading errors:** an **early error propagates and compounds** — one wrong step corrupts everything built on it, and the agent continues on the broken state (the long-horizon compounding problem, Lesson 6, 20).
4. **Context loss:** the agent **loses track of context/state** over a long task — forgets earlier decisions, the goal, or key facts (the memory problem, Lesson 7's finite-context wall).
5. **Tool misuse:** the agent **uses tools incorrectly** — wrong tool, wrong arguments, dangerous calls, calls when it shouldn't (the tool-selection/dynamic-decision problem, Lesson 5/6/13.5).

**Why agentic settings amplify existing AI failures:** the same AI failures (hallucination, bias, errors) become **far worse in agentic settings** because **agents take *actions* over *many steps*** (vs a single text output):
- **Hallucination → harmful action:** in a chatbot, a hallucination is a **wrong statement** (the user can ignore it). In an agent, a hallucination becomes a **harmful action** — the agent *acts* on the false belief (deletes the wrong file, calls the wrong API, takes a destructive step). The failure has **real-world consequences**, not just wrong text.
- **Errors compound over steps:** a single AI error is contained; but an agent runs **many steps**, so an **early error cascades** — the agent builds on the broken result, and the error **propagates and amplifies** through the whole task (cascading errors). A 5% per-step error rate over 20 steps is catastrophic end-to-end (Lesson 6, 20).
- **Autonomy removes the human check:** a chatbot's output is reviewed by the human reading it; an **autonomous agent acts without that per-step human check**, so failures **go through unchecked** until the damage is done.
- **Multi-agent multiplies it** — multiple agents mean more failure surface, inter-agent misalignment, and failures propagating between agents (MASFT's categories).
So **agentic settings amplify existing AI failures** because they convert **wrong beliefs into wrong actions, compound errors across many autonomous steps, and remove the per-step human check** — turning a manageable single-output error into a cascading, consequential, autonomous failure. This is **why agent reliability is so much harder than LLM reliability** (Lesson 31's 'why capable models still fail') — and why the rest of the phase (verification, scope, the workbench) exists to catch these modes before they cause harm.

exercises.title: Detecting and mitigating the recurring failure modes
exercises.prompt: For each of the five recurring failure modes (hallucinated actions, scope creep, cascading errors, context loss, tool misuse), describe a concrete mitigation. Implement a simple failure-mode detector that flags suspected occurrences in an agent run.
exercises.hint: Hallucinated actions → validate tool calls against the registry. Scope creep → scope contracts/diff checks. Cascading errors → verify each step / early stopping. Context loss → durable state/memory. Tool misuse → schema validation + guardrails. Detector: scan the run for unknown tools, out-of-scope file changes, repeated errors, etc.
exercises.solution:
**Mitigations for each recurring failure mode:**
- **Hallucinated actions** → **validate every tool call against the registry before executing** (Phase 13.1, Lesson 6) — reject calls to nonexistent tools or with invalid args (catches hallucinated tools/arguments). Also: ground actions in observed facts (the agent loop's Observation step, Lesson 1), and require the agent to cite evidence for actions.
- **Scope creep** → **scope contracts** (Lesson 36) — a per-task file declaring allowed/forbidden files and acceptance criteria, with a **diff check** that flags changes outside the allowed scope. Turn 'stay in scope' from a wish into a verifiable constraint (Lesson 33's executable constraints).
- **Cascading errors** → **verify each step (or key steps) before building on it** — runtime feedback loops (Lesson 37) so the agent reacts to real results not predictions, **verification gates** (Lesson 38), and **early stopping** when a step fails (don't continue on a broken state). Catch the error *before* it cascades.
- **Context loss** → **durable repo memory and state** (Lesson 34) — store state/decisions in versioned files the agent re-reads each turn (not just volatile chat context), plus memory systems (Lessons 7-9) and handoff packets (Lesson 40). The state survives context-window limits.
- **Tool misuse** → **schema validation + argument coercion + guardrails** (Phase 13.1, Lesson 6, 16) — validate tool calls, coerce args, gate dangerous tools, and good tool-schema design (Phase 13.5) so the agent picks/uses tools correctly.
Each mode has a **targeted, mostly-deterministic mitigation** — and crucially, most are **workbench surfaces** (scope contracts, verification gates, durable state, feedback loops — Lessons 31-42), which is the phase's thesis: **engineer the surfaces that catch these failures.**

**A simple failure-mode detector:**
```python
def detect_failures(run, registry, scope_contract):
    flags = []
    for step in run.steps:
        # HALLUCINATED ACTIONS: tool not in the registry
        if step.tool and step.tool not in registry:
            flags.append(("hallucinated_action", step))
        # TOOL MISUSE: invalid args against the schema
        if step.tool and not validate(step.args, registry.get(step.tool, {})):
            flags.append(("tool_misuse", step))
    # SCOPE CREEP: files changed outside the allowed set
    for f in run.changed_files:
        if f not in scope_contract["allowed_files"]:
            flags.append(("scope_creep", f))
    # CASCADING ERRORS: repeated failures / errors building on errors
    if count_consecutive_errors(run) >= 3:
        flags.append(("cascading_errors", run))
    # CONTEXT LOSS: agent re-asks for info it already had / contradicts prior state
    if detect_contradictions(run.state_history):
        flags.append(("context_loss", run))
    return flags
```
The detector **scans the agent run for symptoms of each mode**: **unknown tools** (hallucinated actions), **invalid args** (tool misuse), **out-of-scope file changes** (scope creep, via the scope contract, Lesson 36), **repeated consecutive errors** (cascading errors), and **contradictions/re-asks in state** (context loss). It's a **deterministic check over the run's artifacts** (steps, changed files, state history) — exactly the kind of **automated failure detection** that feeds into verification gates (Lesson 38) and CI. Running such a detector turns 'hope the agent didn't fail' into 'automatically flag suspected failures' — the diagnostic complement to the mitigations. The detector is heuristic (it flags *suspected* failures for review/blocking), but it catches the **common, recognizable patterns** of the five modes, providing an automated safety net (and the basis for the verification gate and reviewer agent, Lessons 38-39).

exercises.title: Why failure modes are structural, and the case for the workbench
exercises.prompt: Explain why these failure modes are structural (not fixed by a better model alone), how they connect to the phase's central thesis that 'capable models still fail without a workbench' (Lesson 31), the relationship between the failure taxonomy and the workbench surfaces (Lessons 31-42), and why detection + verification + human-in-the-loop is the doctrine rather than prevention.
exercises.hint: Structural: they stem from the agent loop's nature (acting over many steps, autonomously, with finite context) — a better model reduces but doesn't eliminate them. Connects to Lesson 31: capability ≠ reliability; you need the workbench. Each failure mode maps to a workbench surface. Doctrine: you can't PREVENT all failures, so DETECT + VERIFY + human gate (contain, don't prevent).
exercises.solution:
**Why these failure modes are structural (not fixed by a better model alone):** the five modes stem from the **fundamental nature of agentic operation**, not from model deficiencies that scale away:
- **Acting over many steps** → **cascading errors** are structural: any nonzero per-step error rate **compounds** over a long task (a 99% per-step model still fails ~18% of 20-step tasks). A better model lowers the per-step rate but **can't make it zero**, so compounding persists — it's a **mathematical property of multi-step autonomy**, not a model bug.
- **Autonomy (no per-step human check)** → **hallucinated actions and scope creep** go unchecked: the agent acts on its beliefs without review, so a hallucination *becomes an action* and the agent can drift. A better model hallucinates/drifts less but **still does sometimes**, and autonomy means **nothing catches it** unless you build a check. Structural to autonomous operation.
- **Finite context** → **context loss** is structural: the context window is **finite** but tasks/conversations aren't (Lesson 7), so over a long task the agent **must** lose context unless you build memory. A bigger context window helps but **doesn't eliminate** the wall — structural.
- **Tool use under uncertainty** → **tool misuse** is structural: choosing among many tools with imperfect info will **sometimes go wrong** (Lesson 6's dynamic-decision problem). Better models choose better but **not perfectly**.
So **a better model *reduces* these failures but *cannot eliminate* them** — they're **structural consequences of acting autonomously, over many steps, with finite context, under uncertainty**. This is the key insight: **agent reliability is not solved by model capability alone.**

**Connection to 'capable models still fail without a workbench' (Lesson 31):** this is the phase's **central thesis** — and the structural failure modes are *why* it's true. **Capability ≠ reliability:** a frontier model is highly *capable* (it can do the task), but **execution reliability** (does it actually complete the task safely, correctly, in scope, without cascading errors?) is a **separate property** that capability doesn't guarantee. Because the failure modes are **structural** (not fixed by capability), **even a frontier model produces unreliable, unsafe-to-ship work without the surrounding engineering** — the **workbench** (Lesson 31). You can't model-scale your way to reliability; you need **engineered surfaces** that catch the structural failures. The failure taxonomy is the **diagnosis**; the workbench is the **treatment**.

**The failure taxonomy ↔ workbench surfaces (Lessons 31-42) mapping:** the workbench's seven surfaces are **precisely the responses to these structural failure modes** (Exercise 2's mitigations, systematized):
- **Instructions as executable constraints (Lesson 33)** → catches scope creep, forbidden actions (rules become checks).
- **Repo memory and durable state (Lesson 34)** → catches context loss (durable state survives the context wall).
- **Initialization scripts (Lesson 35)** → reduces redundant work and errors per session.
- **Scope contracts (Lesson 36)** → catches scope creep (verifiable task boundaries).
- **Runtime feedback loops (Lesson 37)** → catches cascading errors (react to real results, not predictions).
- **Verification gates (Lesson 38)** → catches all modes (the agent doesn't mark its own work done — task-verification-failure category).
- **Reviewer agent (Lesson 39)** → catches what the builder misses (separate verification — the self-verification ceiling, Lesson 5).
- **Multi-session handoff (Lesson 40)** → catches context loss across sessions.
Each **workbench surface targets specific structural failure modes** — the workbench is the **engineered defense** against the failure taxonomy. (The MASFT categories — specification, inter-agent misalignment, task-verification — map similarly: good instructions/scope fix specification, orchestration fixes misalignment, verification gates fix task-verification.)

**Why detection + verification + human-in-the-loop is the doctrine (not prevention):** because the failure modes are **structural — you cannot *prevent* all of them** (a better model can't eliminate them, Exercise 3's premise). So, exactly like the security doctrine (prompt injection unsolved → contain, Phase 13.15, Lesson 27), the agent-reliability doctrine is **assume failures will occur and catch/contain them:**
- **Detection (Exercise 2):** **automatically detect** suspected failures (the detector) — you can't prevent them, so detect them.
- **Verification (Lessons 38-39):** **verify the work externally** (verification gates, reviewer agent) — don't trust the agent's self-assessment (the self-verification ceiling, Lesson 5, and MASFT's task-verification-failure category). The builder doesn't mark its own work done; an independent verifier does.
- **Human-in-the-loop (Phase 11.9, Lessons 12, 21, 36):** **gate irreversible/high-stakes actions with human approval** — so even when the agent fails (hallucinated/out-of-scope/dangerous action), a human catches it before harm.
This is **'detect and contain', not 'prevent'** — the same realism as security (Phase 13.15): **since you can't make a structurally-fallible agent perfect, you build the surfaces (detection, verification, human gates) that catch its inevitable failures before they cause harm.** The synthesis: the failure modes (MASFT's 14 in 3 categories, the 5 recurring) are **structural consequences of autonomous multi-step operation with finite context** — **not fixable by model capability alone** — which is *why* 'capable models still fail without a workbench' (Lesson 31). The taxonomy is the **diagnosis**, the **workbench surfaces (Lessons 31-42) are the engineered treatment**, and the **doctrine is detect-verify-contain (not prevent)** — because you can't eliminate structural failures, you build the engineering that **catches them before they ship harmful work**. This is the entire case for the agent-workbench mini-track that closes Phase 14: **agent reliability is an engineering discipline (the workbench), not a model property.**
